attributes.stem:
A 10-year-old boy is brought to the Emergency Department by his parents with worsening asthma symptoms over three days following an upper respiratory tract infection. He has a history of asthma for which he takes a regular preventer inhaler and as-needed reliever. His parents report he has been using his reliever inhaler frequently with minimal improvement.

On examination, he appears distressed and is sitting upright, leaning forward. His pulse rate is 125/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 35/min, SpO2 92% on 6 L/min oxygen via face mask, and temperature 37.2°C. He is only able to speak in short phrases. Despite receiving three doses of nebulised salbutamol and ipratropium bromide in the Emergency Department, his condition has not improved significantly.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following findings would be most suggestive of a presentation requiring paediatric intensive care unit admission?
options: Oxygen saturation of 91% on room air
Inability to speak more than single words
attributes.explanation:
Asthma exacerbations in children can rapidly progress to life-threatening situations requiring intensive care management. The ability to recognise signs of severe or life-threatening asthma is crucial for appropriate and timely escalation of care.

Silent chest on auscultation is one of the most concerning findings in asthma exacerbations. It indicates such severe airflow limitation that there is insufficient air movement to generate audible wheezing. This represents critical airway obstruction and impending respiratory arrest. When a patient with asthma develops a silent chest that does not improve with initial treatment, immediate transfer to intensive care is warranted as this finding suggests the patient may soon develop respiratory muscle fatigue and respiratory failure.

Tachycardia of 130/min is common in moderate to severe asthma exacerbations and reflects the body's sympathetic response to respiratory distress. While concerning, tachycardia alone is not specifically an indication for intensive care admission unless it progresses to bradycardia, which would suggest pre-terminal decompensation.

Oxygen saturation of 91% on room air indicates hypoxemia but is not severe enough to warrant intensive care admission on its own. According to guidelines, oxygen saturations below 90% that do not improve with initial management would be more concerning and might necessitate intensive care.

Inability to speak more than single words suggests a severe asthma exacerbation with significant work of breathing. While this indicates a severe attack requiring aggressive management, it is not as ominous as a silent chest. Patients who can only speak in single words still have sufficient air movement to generate speech.

Respiratory rate of 40/min indicates significant respiratory distress but is an expected finding in a child with a severe asthma exacerbation. More concerning would be a decreasing respiratory rate in the setting of worsening clinical status, which might indicate respiratory muscle fatigue and impending respiratory arrest.

In summary, while all these findings indicate a severe asthma exacerbation requiring aggressive management, a silent chest is the most concerning as it suggests critical airway obstruction with minimal air movement and impending respiratory arrest. Other life-threatening signs that would warrant immediate intensive care include drowsiness, confusion, cyanosis, bradycardia, exhaustion, and poor respiratory effort.
learningPoints: Silent chest on auscultation during an asthma exacerbation indicates severe airflow obstruction and is a sign of impending respiratory arrest requiring immediate intensive care.
Bradycardia, bradypnoea, and lethargy in a child with asthma are ominous signs indicating severe respiratory compromise.
Paediatric ICU admission criteria for asthma include poor respiratory effort, silent chest, drowsiness, exhaustion, cyanosis, or oxygen saturation <90% despite initial management.
references: Global Initiative for Asthma. Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention, 2020. Available at: www.ginasthma.org
National Asthma Council Australia. Australian Asthma Handbook, Version 2.0 [online]. National Asthma Council Australia, Melbourne, 2019. Available at: http://asthmahandbook.org.au

Marshall P, Possick J, Chupp G. Intensive care unit management of status asthmaticus. Clin Pulm Med. 2009;16:293-301. doi:10.1097/CPM.0b013e3181bdff38. Available at: https://journals.lww.com/clinpulm/abstract/2009/11000/intensive_care_unit_management_of_status.1.aspx
